entries.ocr_text:
PROCLAMATION
TO ALL TO WHOM THESE PRESENTS SHALL COME :
WHEREAS , the present structure of the state and local govern-
ment of Kentucky obstructs the economic and ef-
fective management of state and local business ; and
WHEREAS , bringing together the multiplicity of scattered
boards , commissions , departments and other agen-
cies of the state and local government into a sys-
tematic and orderly plan is essential to effective
administration at minimum expense ; and
WHEREAS , preparation of a budget to provide for the economi-
cal financing of state and local services is depen-
dent on prior reorganization of the state and local
government ;
NOW , THEREFORE , in order to insure the people of the
Commonwealth a reorganized and efficient plan
of government , and in accordance with the power
vested in me by Section 80 of the Constitution of
Kentucky to convene the General Assembly into
Extraordinary Session, as Governor of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , I, A. B. Chandler , do issue
this , my proclamation , convening the General As-
sembly of Kentucky in Extraordinary Session at the
seat of government at Frankfort, Kentucky , at 7
p.m. , CST on Monday, February 27, 1956 , for the
sole purpose of considering the following subject :
( 1) To pass acts reorganizing and simplify-
ing the state and local government .
You will therefore , in accordance with this proclamation ,
assemble in Extraordinary Session at the seat of government
at Frankfort , Kentucky , on the day and hour above written , to
consider the subject herein mentioned , and only said subject .
Done at Frankfort, Kentucky , this the 23rd
day of February in the year of our Lord , one
thousand nine hundred and fifty six, and in the
year of the Commonwealth the one hundred and
sixty- fourth .
( Signed ) A. B. CHANDLER , Governor
( Signed ) Thelma L. Stovall
Secretary of State
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Special Session, 1956, p. 11

entries.ocr_text:
ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE EXTRAORDINARY SESSION OF THE GENERAL
ASSEMBLY , WHICH WAS BEGUN IN THE CITY OF FRANKFORT ,
KENTUCKY, ON MONDAY , FEBRUARY THE TWENTY - SEVENTH ,
AND ENDED ON THURSDAY , MARCH THE EIGHTH , 1956
CHAPTER 1
(S.B. 1 )
AN ACT to amend the Southern Regional Education Compact to in-
crease the number of members of the Board of Control for Southern
Regional Education from four from each state to five from each
state and to specify that one member from each state shall be
appointed by the Governor from among the membership of the
legislature of that state .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That part of paragraph 2 of the Southern Re-
gional Education Compact which reads :
"The states do further hereby establish and create a joint
agency which shall be known as the Board of Control for
Southern Regional Education ( hereinafter referred to as the
"board " ) , the members of which board shall consist of the
Governor of each state , ex officio , and three additional citizens
of each state to be appointed by the Governor thereof , at least
one of whom shall be selected from the field of education ."
Shall be amended to read as follows :
"The states do further hereby establish and created a joint
agency which shall be known as the Board of Control for South-
ern Regional Education (hereinafter referred to as the "board "),
the members of which board shall consist of the Governor of
each state , ex officio , and four additional citizens of each state to
be appointed by the Governor thereof , at least one of whom
shall be selected from the field of education , and at least one
of whom shall be a member of the legislature of that state . "
Section 2. This amendment will take effect when eight or
more of the states party to the compact have given legislative
approval to the amendment .
Approved March 12 , 1956
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Special Session, 1956, p. 12

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Southern Regional Education Compact
classification.summary: This act amends the Southern Regional Education Compact by increasing the number of members on the Board of Control for Southern Regional Education from four to five per state. The amendment specifies that one of the additional members must be appointed by the Governor from the state legislature.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns an amendment to the Southern Regional Education Compact, specifically regarding the composition of its Board of Control. The amendment increases the number of members from each state and mandates that one member be from the state legislature. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates the nature of the amendment without any racial implications.

entries.ocr_text:
8 ACTS , FIRST EXTRAORDINARY SESSION
CHAPTER 2
(S.B. 2)
AN ACT relating to circuit courts .
WHEREAS , the General Assembly has given due regard to the ele-
ments of territory , business and population involved in the division
of the state into judicial districts , and,
WHEREAS , with such regard , the General Assembly has found the
following facts to exist :
(1 ) The entire population of the state as of the last federal census
is 2,944,806 .
(2) That upon the basis of population , additional judicial districts
may be established to the forty -five judicial districts now existing ,
pursuant to Sections 132 and 138 of the Constitution .
(3) That the counties of Grayson , Breckinridge and Meade have a
combined population of 42,013 as of the last federal census .
(4) That the population of Hardin county was 50,312 as of the
last federal census and its population is rapidly increasing .
(5) That currently and heretofore the amount of business in the
Hardin circuit court is heavy and there is a considerable backlog
of cases pending before said court at this time and the business of
the Hardin circuit court combined with the business of the Grayson ,
Breckinridge and Meade circuit courts makes it difficult to prop-
erly take care of the business of all these circuit courts , the com-
bined business of the Hardin , Grayson , Breckinridge and Meade
circuit courts as the Ninth Judicial District as now existing is too
much , and these counties should be re-arranged and re -grouped .
(6 ) That the counties of Grayson , Meade and Breckinridge join
each other .
(7) That Hardin county has a large volume of cases from the
Fort Knox Military Reservation .
(8) That it will be convenient and advantageous from the stand-
point of business , territory and population and will comply with
the spirit and intent of the Constitution and expedite the business
of the courts , and make it more convenient to the citizens of the
counties involved , to create a new judicial district for the counties
of Grayson , Breckinridge and Meade , and to change the Ninth
Judicial District so that it will be composed of Hardin county .
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 23.040 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The state is divided into forty-six circuit court districts ,
each to be composed of the following counties :
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Special Session, 1956, p. 13

classification.title: AN ACT relating to circuit courts.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 23.040 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to reconfigure judicial districts. It creates a new judicial district for Grayson, Breckinridge, and Meade counties, and designates Hardin county as the Ninth Judicial District. The rationale provided is based on population, territory, business volume, and convenience, particularly noting the heavy caseload in Hardin county due to cases from Fort Knox.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative reorganization of judicial districts based on population, territory, and caseload. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' because it amends an existing section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS 23.040).

Section 3. The present circuit judge and Commonwealth's
attorney for the Ninth District as now constituted , shall retain
their respective offices for the time for which they were elected ,
for the Ninth District as it is changed under this Act ; provided ,
however , that if either of them is not on the effective date of
this Act a resident of Hardin county , he shall not be allowed
to retain his respective office , unless he shall remove his resi-
dence , within a reasonable time , into Hardin county .
Section 4. The office of circuit judge and Commonwealth's
attorney for the new judicial district as established under this
Act are hereby created . Until the regular election for circuit
judges and Commonwealth's attorneys , said offices shall be
filled as provided in Section 152 of the Constitution for offices
in which there is a vacancy .
Section 5. If any provision or portion of this Act , or the
application of such provisions or portions to any judicial district
affected hereby should be held invalid, the remainder of this
Act , and the application of such remaining provisions shall not
be affected thereby .
Section 6. Whereas , there is a considerable backlog of
cases pending before the Hardin circuit court and the circuit
courts of the counties of Grayson , Breckinridge , and Meade , an
emergency is declared to exist and this Act shall become effec-

classification.title: AN ACT relating to public welfare services and public welfare departments in cities of the first class and in counties containing such a city.
classification.summary: This act allows fiscal courts in counties with a first-class city to establish a department of welfare governed by specific provisions. It also addresses the continuation of offices for the circuit judge and Commonwealth's attorney in the Ninth District, with a residency requirement for Hardin county, and creates new judicial districts. The act declares an emergency due to a backlog of cases in Hardin circuit court and surrounding counties, making it effective immediately upon passage and approval.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative acts. The first act (CHAPTER 311) deals with the restructuring of judicial districts and the continuation of judicial offices, including a residency requirement for Hardin county. The second act (CHAPTER 3) relates to the establishment of public welfare departments in counties with first-class cities. Neither of these acts explicitly or implicitly mentions race or racial segregation. Therefore, they are not classified as Jim Crow laws. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the 'CHAPTER' and 'AN ACT' formatting, and the approval date. Confidence is high as there are no indicators of racial discrimination.

( b ) Such agreement may be revoked at the option of either
party by a resolution or ordinance or order of the revoking party
filed in the office of the county clerk , and a copy shall be delivered
to the other party . Such revocation shall become effective at
the end of the fiscal year occurring at least one year following
the filing of said resolution or order . In the absence of such
revocation so filed, the agreement shall continue indefinitely .
( c ) The transfer of property and records of other state or
local public welfare activities shall be according to the terms of
the agreement .
Section 6. The fiscal court shall appoint a bipartisan
county welfare advisory board of not less than five nor more than
nine residents of the county . The members shall be appointed in
such a manner that the term of at least one member shall expire
annually . Any vacancy shall be filled for the unexpired term in the
same manner as the original appointment . Members of the
county welfare advisory board shall serve without compensation .
The county welfare advisory board shall suggest
rules and regulations to the county director of welfare with re-
spect to the organization of the county department of welfare ,
and shall advise the director and the fiscal court with respect
to budget estimates , policies and other problems of welfare
activities .

classification.title: Amendments to Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the Department of Education and Rehabilitation Services
classification.summary: This statute amends several sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. Section 2 amends KRS 156.095 to authorize the State Department of Education to establish and direct a statewide program of in-service teacher training. Section 3 amends KRS 156.140 regarding the supervision and appointment of personnel within the Department of Education. Section 4 establishes the Kentucky Industries for the Blind within the Bureau of Rehabilitation Services to provide training and employment for adult blind persons. Section 5 transfers assets and functions from the Kentucky Workshop for the Blind to the Kentucky Industries for the Blind. Section 6 amends KRS 160.210 concerning the election of school board members in independent and county school districts.

It also allows for provisional and temporary appointments under specific conditions. The statute aims to ensure fair and competent selection for state employment.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the process for civil service examinations and appointments within the state government. It focuses on the qualifications, testing methods, and exemptions for various positions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses employment procedures without any racial undertones. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' based on the chapter numbering and the context of legislative acts.

classification.title: An Act relating to the Department of Personnel and the Department of Finance.
classification.summary: This act outlines the functions of the Department of Personnel in establishing and maintaining a classification plan for state service positions, including duties, qualifications, promotion lines, and salary ranges. It also details the process for creating employment lists, making appointments, and notifying the department of changes in employee status. The Department of Finance is empowered to demand supporting information to ensure legal employment and proper compensation for state officers and employees. The act also grants appointing officers the authority to remove subordinates and requires notification to the Department of Personnel, with provisions for filing reasons for removal upon request.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative procedures for state personnel management, including classification, hiring, promotion, and employee removal. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language is purely administrative and procedural. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Acts of the First Extraordinary Session of the General Assembly of Kentucky, 1956
classification.summary: This section of the 1956 Acts details the creation of a Division of Farm Management within the Department of Welfare. It outlines the appointment of a director, the personnel needs, and the functions of the division, which include providing consultation and management services for state institutional farms, acting as a clearinghouse for information and resources, and planning farm programs. An Advisory Board of Farm Management is also established, composed of the Commissioners of Welfare, Mental Health, Conservation, and Agriculture, to advise the division director on long-range planning for land usage, manpower, and production goals.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the establishment and functions of a Division of Farm Management within the Department of Welfare. It details the roles of the director and an advisory board, and the services the division will provide to state institutional farms. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices in the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: CHAPTER 741
classification.summary: This statute establishes the Department of Public Relations and outlines its functions in promoting and publicizing the Commonwealth of Kentucky, its resources, and attractions. It details the department's authority to engage in various advertising and publicity activities, including the preparation and distribution of materials like road maps, booklets, and motion pictures. The statute also transfers functions, property, and funds from the Division of Publicity of the Department of Conservation to the new Department of Public Relations and repeals KRS Section 146.060. Additionally, it repeals Sections 147.260 through 147.310 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, which were related to the Department of Economic Development.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the creation and functions of the Department of Public Relations, focusing on promoting the state's resources and attractions. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. The statute's purpose is economic development and tourism promotion. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The act details the appointment and responsibilities of the Commissioner, who will oversee the department's operations and programs.

classification.title: CHAPTER 743
classification.summary: This statute establishes the Agricultural Development Board and the Industrial Development Board within a department, outlining their composition, appointment, and responsibilities. The Agricultural Development Board, composed of up to twenty-five non-partisan members appointed by the Governor, is tasked with determining policies to attract favorable attention to the Commonwealth's agricultural potentialities and facilities. The Industrial Development Board, also with up to twenty-five members appointed by the Governor, is responsible for preparing and executing research and advertising programs to promote industrial and commercial potentialities, and for assisting and coordinating efforts of various state and local entities in promoting industry and mining.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the creation and functions of agricultural and industrial development boards. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed during a legislative session. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to Jim Crow legislation.

It also details the information the department should obtain from interested enterprises.

classification.title: An Act relating to the Department of Public Safety.
classification.summary: This act transfers various functions from the Department of Kentucky State Police, Department of Insurance, State Fire Marshal, and Department of Revenue to the newly established or reorganized Department of Public Safety. It details the transfer of records, property, personnel, and appropriations related to these functions, including those concerning the Kentucky State Police, fire loss, and motor vehicle licensing and financial responsibility.

classification.title: CHAPTER 747, Section 4, 5, and 6
classification.summary: This statute reorganizes the Kentucky State Police, abolishing the Department of Kentucky State Police and the office of Commissioner of Kentucky State Police, and establishing a Division of Kentucky State Police headed by a Director. It also repeals certain sections of KRS 16 and amends KRS 16.070 to allow the Commissioner of Public Safety to lease motor vehicles and equipment, and for the Commissioner of Highways and Commissioner of Public Safety to agree on services and repairs for state vehicles. The statute focuses on administrative and operational changes within the state police and highway departments.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details administrative changes within the Kentucky State Police and the Department of Highways. It focuses on the restructuring of departments, the roles of directors and commissioners, and the acquisition and maintenance of state vehicles and equipment. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: ACTS, FIRST EXTRAORDINARY SESSION 1956
classification.summary: This section of the Acts of the First Extraordinary Session of 1956 details the organization and functions of the Division of Fire Prevention and the Division of Operator Responsibility within the Department of Public Safety. It amends KRS 227.200 to define terms related to fire loss and outlines the responsibilities of the State Fire Marshal and the Commissioner of Public Safety. It also specifies the delegation of functions related to motor vehicle operator licensing from the Department of Revenue to the Division of Operator Responsibility.

CHAPTER 7
49

Responsibility shall exercise all the functions heretofore exercised by the Department of Revenue provided in KRS, Chapter 187, relating to the administration and enforcement of the financial responsibility law. Any reference in KRS, Chapter 187, to the Department of Revenue or the "department," henceforth shall be construed to be a reference to the Division of Operator Responsibility, except where otherwise specified herein.

Section 10. Section 186.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:

(1) "Department," as used in KRS 186.015 to 186.270, means the Department of Revenue. As used in KRS 186.400 to 186.640, it means the Department of Public Safety.

(2) "Highway" means every way or place of whatever nature when any part of it is open to the use of the public, as a matter of right, license or privilege, for the purpose of vehicular traffic.

(3) "Manufacturer" means any person engaged in manufacturing motor vehicles who will, under normal conditions during the year, manufacture or assemble at least ten new motor vehicles.

(4) "Motor vehicle," as used in KRS 186.020 to 186.260, includes all vehicles, as defined in paragraph (a) of subsection (7) of this section, which are propelled otherwise than by muscular power. As used in KRS 186.400 to 186.640, it means all vehicles, as defined in paragraph (b) of subsection (7) of this section, which are self-propelled.

(5) "Operator" means any person in actual control of a motor vehicle upon a highway.

(6) "Owner" means a person who holds the legal title of a vehicle. If a vehicle is the subject of an agreement for the conditional sale or lease, with the right of purchase upon performance of the conditions stated in the agreement and with an immediate right of possession vested in the conditional vendee or lessee, or if a mortgagor of a vehicle is entitled to possession, the conditional vendee or lessee or mortgagor shall be deemed the owner.

(7) (a) "Vehicle," as used in KRS 186.020 to 186.260, includes all agencies for the transportation of persons or property over or upon the public highways of this Commonwealth

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 186.010 concerning definitions for motor vehicle laws
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 186.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to clarify the definitions of 'Department', 'Highway', 'Manufacturer', 'Motor vehicle', 'Operator', 'Owner', and 'Vehicle' as used in various chapters of KRS related to motor vehicles and operator responsibility. It specifies that 'Department' refers to the Department of Revenue or the Department of Public Safety depending on the KRS chapter context.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to existing Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) that defines terms related to motor vehicles and their operators. It reassigns responsibilities between departments and clarifies definitions. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute amends Section 186.400 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, which pertains to vehicle definitions and the administration of related provisions. It clarifies what constitutes a "vehicle" for the purposes of KRS 186.400 to 186.640, excluding certain types of construction equipment, rail vehicles, and electric-powered vehicles within municipalities. It also assigns administrative responsibilities for these statutes to the Department of Public Safety and the Department of Revenue, and outlines the provision of necessary forms.

classification.summary: This statute amends Section 186.490 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, outlining the duties of circuit clerks in issuing operator's licenses. It also specifies penalties for failure to report and remit funds on time.

classification.title: An Act relating to motor vehicles and operators thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes by repealing Sections 186.575 and 281.725. It also establishes requirements for owners and operators of motor vehicles used for hire, including driver age restrictions (18 for property transport, 21 for passenger transport) and the necessity of obtaining a certificate from the Department of Public Safety attesting to the driver's health, vision, sobriety, intelligence, care, ability, and experience. Circuit clerks are tasked with issuing these certificates and badges, reporting to the Department of Public Safety, and maintaining records.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on regulations for motor vehicles used for hire, specifically concerning driver qualifications and age. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The requirements apply universally to all drivers of vehicles for hire. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to Section 43.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 43.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the Auditor of Public Accounts. It details the appointment and duties of an Assistant Auditor of Public Accounts, who must be a certified public accountant and a resident of the state for at least two years. The assistant auditor will supervise technical work and assist the Auditor, and will assume the Auditor's duties in case of absence, incapacity, or vacancy. The Auditor is also permitted to employ other subordinate personnel, subject to KRS 12.060.

classification.title: CHAPTER 759
classification.summary: This statute addresses the admission of Negroes to educational institutions in Kentucky. It states that Negroes will not be admitted on an equal basis with other races and that no Negro citizen will be forced to attend a segregated regional institution if an institution offering the same course of study to other races exists within the Commonwealth. It also outlines a program for medical research and education, allowing contracts with medical schools to admit residents of the Commonwealth, with specific provisions for payment and reporting. Several sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are repealed.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'Negroes' and discusses their admission to educational institutions on a basis unequal to 'other races'. This directly indicates a racial segregation policy. The context of the time period (1956) and the mention of 'segregated regional institution' further solidify its classification as a Jim Crow-era law. The statute's focus on education and its explicit racial language make it a clear example of a Jim Crow law in the education sector. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session's proceedings (Gen Assemb Special 1956.pdf).

PROCLAMATION
TO ALL TO WHOM THESE PRESENTS SHALL COME :
WHEREAS , the industrial development of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky is directly dependent upon an ade-
quate system of highway transportation ; and ,
WHEREAS , the present highway transportation system is, by
reasons of existing limitations , inadequate to the
needs of an expanding economy and is detrimental
to the location and expansion of industries in
the Commonwealth of Kentucky ; and ,
WHEREAS , the system of highways in Kentucky is inadequate
to meet the demands of present day needs and
unsafe for modern travel ; and ,
WHEREAS , it is necessary to increase the revenues necessary
to the building of a modern system of highways ;
and ,
WHEREAS , it is essential that the Department of Highways
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky consult and
advise with the fiscal courts of the various coun-
ties on the subject of construction and improve-
ment of secondary and rural roads lying within
certain counties :
NOW , THEREFORE , in order to accomplish the objectives
hereinabove set out , and in accordance with the
power vested in me by the Constitution of Ken-
tucky to convene the General Assembly in Extra-
ordinary Session, as Governor of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , I, A. B. Chandler , do issue
this , my proclamation , convening the General As-
sembly of Kentucky in Extraordinary Session at
the seat of government at Frankfort, Kentucky ,
at 2 p.m. ( CST ) on Friday , March 9, 1956, for
the sole purpose of considering the following sub-
jects :
( 1) An Act to provide transportation by motor
vehicles over highways of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky and prescribing the permissible weight ,
height and length thereof .

classification.title: Proclamation for Extraordinary Session of the General Assembly, March 8, 1956
classification.summary: This document is a proclamation by Governor A.B. Chandler calling for an extraordinary session of the Kentucky General Assembly on March 8, 1956. The session was to consider specific legislative matters related to taxation for the Road Fund, motor transportation, a $100,000,000 bond issue for road and bridge construction, the selection of secondary and rural roads, and the control of Johnson grass on state rights-of-way. The proclamation explicitly states that the assembly would consider only these mentioned subjects.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a proclamation calling for a special legislative session. The listed subjects for consideration are all related to infrastructure, transportation, and land management (road fund taxes, motor vehicles, road construction bonds, road selection, and Johnson grass control). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Proclamation Convening the General Assembly in Extraordinary Session, March 8, 1956
classification.summary: This document is a proclamation by Governor A. B. Chandler of Kentucky, convening the General Assembly in an extraordinary session on March 8, 1956. The session's purpose was to consider six specific subjects, all related to motor highways, roads, bridges, motor transportation, and taxation for the Road Fund. The proclamation details the subjects to be considered, including increasing permissible weight for motor highways, construction of roads and bridges in conjunction with Johnson City, Tennessee, selection of secondary and trunk roads, issuance of bonds for road construction, motor transportation operations, and imposition of taxes for the Road Fund. Several of these acts were to become effective only after approval by the people in the general election of November 6, 1956.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a proclamation from the Governor of Kentucky convening the General Assembly for an extraordinary session. The listed subjects for consideration are exclusively related to infrastructure, transportation, and taxation. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would indicate this is a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is a proclamation calling for legislative action. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned.

classification.title: CHAPTER 385
classification.summary: This statute authorizes the issuance of one hundred million dollars ($100,000,000) in bonds for the Commonwealth of Kentucky. It details the denominations, printing, signing, interest rates (not exceeding three percent per annum), and maturity dates of these bonds. The bonds are dated January 1, 1957, with semiannual interest payments on January 1 and July 1. Coupons for semiannual interest will bear the facsimile signatures of the Governor and Treasurer. The bonds and coupons are payable in lawful U.S. money. Owners can register bonds for principal and interest with the Department of Finance for a fee. All bonds and their interest are exempt from state, county, and municipal taxation. The State Property and Buildings Commission will determine the form of the bonds and the method of registration, and the cost of printing will be paid from the State Road Fund. The statute also requires approval from the people of Kentucky via a general election on November 6, 1956.

The confidence is high because the content is clearly financial and administrative, with no discernible racial component.

The commission is also empowered to invest sinking fund amounts and deposit them in banks.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The act also specifies the annual registration deadline of March 1st.

Section 4. If an owner because of sickness or absence from
the state is unable to renew the registration of his trailer , semi-
trailer or house - trailer on or before March 1, he will be deemed
to have complied with the law, if before operating the trailer ,
semi -trailer or house-trailer in this state he registers it at some
later date and pays the fee for the full year , just as though he
had renewed the registration on or before March 1. The owner
shall file with the application an affidavit showing sickness or
absence .
Section 5. The application blank shall contain blank spaces
for , and the applicant shall fill in the owner's name and post
office address , make of the trailer , semi -trailer or house - trailer ,
and serial or identification number . Every application shall be
accompanied by the appropriate fee .
Section 6. Upon filing application and paying the fee as
provided by law, said owner shall be given a receipt containing
the registration number and the information included in the
application and one plate for the registration year bearing said
number .
Section 7. The annual registration fee for trailers and semi-
trailers which are drawn by motor vehicles required to be licensed
under section 186.050 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes shall be
$ 4.50 . The provisions of this Act shall not apply to privately
owned and operated trailers used for the transportation of boats ,
luggage , personal effects , farm products , farm supplies or farm
equipment .
The registration fee for house-trailers shall be $9.50.
The clerk shall issue the registration plate furnished by the
department and shall be paid for this service the sum of fifty
cents .

that reason registers for only part of a year the registration fee

classification.title: CHAPTER 7 101
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for granting certificates for motor carriers. It details the conditions under which an application for a certificate will be granted, including the applicant's ability to perform the service, the inadequacy of existing transportation, and the public convenience and necessity. It also specifies how certificates should be granted, including the designation of routes and territories, and the limitation on the number of taxicabs. A carrier is entitled to multiple certificates to cover their authorized operation. Permits will be granted to qualified contract carriers.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 281.650 regarding motor vehicle certificates and permits
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 281.650 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the renewal of motor vehicle certificates or permits. It specifies that renewals must occur before January 1st of each year, outlines the process for renewal applications, states that vehicles operated after January 1st must have the requisite fee paid, and declares that certificates or permits not renewed within one calendar year after the renewal date become null and void, though a new application can be filed. It also notes that the department shall not renew any certificate or permit under certain unspecified conditions.

classification.title: An Act relating to motor carriers and taxicabs.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Chapter 281 concerning motor carriers and taxicabs. It addresses temporary approval for consolidation or merger of motor carrier properties, tax proration for vehicles subject to KRS 281.813, and clarifies that the department can grant certificates for taxicab operations from unincorporated communities. It also prohibits taxicabs from competing with common carriers outside city or unincorporated community limits, except for casual trips where passengers cannot be picked up along the route.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the excise tax on gasoline.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 138.240 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the excise tax on gasoline. It requires dealers to submit a monthly report to the Department of Revenue detailing the total number of gallons of gasoline received in the state during the preceding calendar month. The report must include itemized statements of gasoline produced, refined, manufactured, or compounded by the dealer in the state, as well as gasoline received from other sources, with specific details about each shipment.

The amendment specifies the data required for various types of shipments, such as transport truck, tank car, or cargo lots.

regarding each separate shipment , the date of shipment , the
number of gallons at sixty degrees Fahrenheit in each shipment ,
the name of owner and license number of truck if shipped by
transport truck , the initial and number of tank car if shipped by
rail , the name and owner of barge if shipped by water , the name

and point of destination , and the name of carrier to whom de-
livered for transportation to destination .
( 3 ) There shall be allowed a monthly deduction for evap-
oration , shrinkage or unaccountable losses while in storage , of
that number of gallons equal to the actual loss of gasoline so
sustained out of the total number of gallons of gasoline stored
in any marine terminal , refinery or pipeline terminal , except that
such deduction may not in any event exceed three- fourths of one
percent of the total number of gallons of gasoline stored in any
marine terminal , refinery or pipeline terminal . The remaining
gasoline placed in storage must be fully accounted for as in
physical inventory , accountable loss, withdrawn for export or
withdrawn from storage and received for taxable purposes .
( 4 ) The number of gallons of gasoline added to marine ,
pipeline or refinery storage shall be determined by the depart-
ment by actual measurement of terminal storage tanks in the
manner it deems necessary .

(1) The department may audit the books and records of

after the receipt of notice , an action may be instituted by the de-
partment in the Franklin circuit court and the department shall

ment may waive this requirement and accept the check of the

138.560 to 138.640 , inclusive .
Section 12. Section 138.625 of the Kentucky Revised

or pay the tax at the time provided in KRS 138.590 , a penalty of
twenty percent of the tax and interest at six percent per annum
from the date when due shall be paid on both the tax and the
penalty .

section ( 1) of this section submits in writing to the department
his reasons for failure to comply with KRS 138.560 to 138.640 ,
and if the department finds the reasons sufficient evidence or
justifiable cause for modifying the penalty provided in sub-
section ( 1) of this section , it may modify the penalty exacted
therein to five percent of the amount of the tax due and de-
linquent , provided the five percent penalty may be reduced to

Section 17. Section 138.665 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(1 ) A license shall be required of each motor carrier or
heavy equipment motor carrier before he uses or continues to
use gasoline on the public highways of this state .
(2) Application for a license shall be made to the De-
partment of Revenue on forms prepared and furnished by the
department . The application shall contain such information
as the department deems necessary .
(3) Concurrently with the filing of an application for a
license, the motor carrier or heavy equipment motor carrier
shall file with the department a bond as required under KRS
138.670 . No license shall be issued to any person unless such
person has furnished a bond as provided in KRS 138.670 to
secure payment of taxes , penalties and interest imposed by
this Act .
(4) The application in proper form having been accepted
for filing , the bond having been accepted and approved and
the other conditions and requirements of this section having
been complied with, the department shall issue a license . How-
ever, if an application for a license is filed by any person whose
license has at any time previously been revoked for cause by
the department , or if the department is of the opinion that the
person who makes the application as a subterfuge for the real
party in interest whose license , prior to the time of filing the
application , has been revoked for cause , or that the application
is not for any other reason filed in good faith or is not for
sufficient cause , the department may , after a hearing of which
the applicant shall be given ten days' notice in writing and
in which he shall have the right to appear in person or by
counsel and present testimony , refuse to issue a license to that
person .

day of doing business as a dealer without an uncancelled license
shall be a separate offense .
(10 ) Any person who willfully violates any of the pro-

in the county jail for not more than one year , or both .

KRS 138.460 or 138.470 shall be fined not less than twenty - five
dollars nor more than one thousand dollars . If the offender
is an individual , he may be imprisoned in the county jail for
not more than six months , or both so fined and imprisoned .
(14 ) Any person who violates any of the provisions of
KRS 138.480 or 138.490 shall be fined not more than one
thousand dollars or imprisoned in the county jail for not more
than thirty days , or both .

( 2 ) , ( 7 ) , ( 8 ) , ( 9 ) , ( 11 ) or ( 14) of this section is a corporation ,
the principal officer or the officer directly responsible for the
violation , or both , may be imprisoned as provided in those
subsections .

by the commission , shall be guilty of a misdemeanor , and upon
conviction thereof shall be fined not less than one hundred

entries.ocr_text:
PROCLAMATION
TO ALL TO WHOM THESE PRESENTS SHALL COME :
WHEREAS , the budgetary needs of the government have not
yet been fully determined by the General Assem-
bly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky ; and
WHEREAS , such budgetary needs must be determined before
the amount of revenue to meet such needs can be
ascertained;
NOW, THEREFORE , in order to determine the budgetary
needs of government , and according to the power
vested in me by the Constitution of Kentucky to
convene the General Assembly into Extraordinary
Session, I do hereby issue this , my proclamation ,
convening the General Assembly of Kentucky in
Extraordinary Session at the seat of government
at Frankfort , Kentucky at 11:00 o'clock AM, on
Thursday , March 29 , 1956 , for the sole purpose
of considering the following subject :
( 1 ) To make appropriations for the support of
government and thereby fix the budgetary require-
ments of the government of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky .
You will therefore , in accordance with this proclamation ,
assemble in Extraordinary Session at the seat of government
at Frankfort , Kentucky , on the day and date above written , to
consider the subject herein mentioned , and only said subject .
Done at Frankfort , Kentucky , this 28th day of
March in the year of our Lord , one thousand nine
hundred and fifty -six, and in the year of the Com-
monwealth , the one hundred and sixty -fourth .
( Signed ) A. B. CHANDLER , Governor
( Signed ) Thelma L. Stovall
Secretary of State

It also outlines the transfer of certain functions from the Department of State Police to the Department of Public Safety, effective September 1, 1956, as per House Bill No. 1, First Extraordinary Session 1956. Appropriations for the Department of Aeronautics and the Department of Economic Development (including General Operations, Mineral Survey, Topographic Mapping, and Water Resources Survey) are also listed.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details budgetary allocations and departmental reorganizations within the Kentucky state government for the fiscal years 1956-57 and 1957-58. It mentions the transfer of functions between the Department of State Police and the Department of Public Safety, referencing another bill (House Bill No. 1, First Extraordinary Session 1956). There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices or segregation. The content is purely administrative and financial. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Revised Statute 157.360 , subsection ( 6 ) , only for personnel who

Board of Education . The State Board of Education may desig-

classification.title: CHAPTER 1157
classification.summary: This statute outlines the allocation of classroom units for public schools in Kentucky for the 1956-57 and 1957-58 school years. It details conditions for allotting basic classroom units based on teacher pay, pupil-teacher ratios in elementary and secondary grades, and minimum pupil-teacher ratios per building. It also specifies the allocation of units for superintendents, principals, special instructional personnel, vocational education, and exceptional children, with specific numerical limits for each category. The statute also addresses the allotment for one-teacher schools with fewer than 25 pupils.

Facilities that are used to house school children shall be
approved by the Superintendent of Public Instruction under
Kentucky Revised Statute 162.060 and 162.160 . No alterations
or additions of old buildings shall be made without prior approval
of the Superintendent of Public Instruction . No space shall be
rented or purchased and converted to classroom use without
prior approval of the Superintendent of Public Instruction . The
State Board of Education may adopt regulations for the approval
on an annual basis of temporary facilities for the housing of
school children but these regulations shall not permit the division
of any present room by partitions of non -structural material . No
classroom units shall be allotted for a second teacher in the
same classroom . No facilities shall be approved for classroom
use which are detrimental to the health of or dangerous to the
safety of school children .
If a school district operates any school or grade of a school
for a term of less than nine scholastic months or for a school day
of less than the minimum required by Kentucky Revised Statute
158.060 , the average daily attendance of such school shall be
reduced proportionately by the Superintendent of Public In-
struction in determining the allotment of classroom units pro-
vided for under Kentucky Revised Statute 157.360 , subsection
( 2 ) except as provided for by regulations of the State Board of
Education .
If at the time of the tentative allotment of school funds
under Kentucky Revised Statutes 157.310-157.440 it shall be
determined that insufficient revenue is available to finance in
full the foundation program including a provision that no dis-
trict receive less than $ 80 per child in average daily attendance
and no district receive less total state aid than it received from
the Per Capita Fund in 1955-56 , the Superintendent of Public
Instruction shall not allot any classroom units under Kentucky
Revised Statute 157.360 , subsection ( 3 ) .

pended pursuant to such section during such fiscal year , and

ing fiscal year ; and provided further , that no expenditure shall

beginning on the date of enactment hereof . Any unexpended
balance at the end of the first fiscal year of the biennium shall
carry over to the second year ; and any unexpended balance at

ment of this act shall revert to the Unemployment Trust Fund
at such time , or at such earlier date as is practicable if for any

or cannot be expended for the purpose set out above.

A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION appropriating money to the Depart-
ment of Finance for restoring the executive mansion and repairing
and replacing the furnishings in the executive mansion .
WHEREAS , the furniture and furnishings within the executive mansion
are in a state of disrepair , and it is necessary to replace many items ;
and
WHEREAS , the executive mansion is the property of the people of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky ; provided by them for the residence of
their elected chief executive officer ; and
WHEREAS , the people of the Commonwealth of Kentucky expect the
executive and legislative departments of state government to take
necessary action to preserve their investment in the executive man-
sion , as well as other property owned by the state and its people,
NOW, THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
There is hereby appropriated to the Department of Finance
for the 1956-57 fiscal year the sum of twenty five thousand
dollars ( $25,000 ) to be used for restoring the Executive Mansion
and for repairing and replacing furniture and furnishings in the
Executive Mansion .
Approved April 11 , 1956

182 ACTS , THIRD EXTRAORDINARY SESSION
CHAPTER 3
(S.R. 6)
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION appropriating money to Elections-
For County Expenses ( state share ) , and declaring and emergency .
WHEREAS , there is not sufficient money appropriated to the account
for the state's share of county election expenses due to the change
in the date of the primary election ; and
WHEREAS , the counties do not have funds available to pay election ex-
penses due to the change in the primary election date ;
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. There is hereby appropriated to the Elections-
County Expenses ( state share ) account the sum of $165,000.00
to be used in reimbursing the county treasury of each county
for a portion of the expense of conducting the primary election
to be held May 29, 1956. The county treasurer in each county
shall certify to the Department of Finance the number of pre-
cincts in his county . Upon proper certification by the county
treasurer of the number of precincts in his county the Depart-
ment of Finance shall advance to each county treasurer the sum
of forty dollars per precinct .
Section 2. Whereas , there is an immediate need for money
to be made available in order that it can be advanced to county
treasurers for primary election expenses , an emergency is hereby
declared to exist and this Resolution shall take effect upon
its
passage and approval by the Governor
.
Approved April
11, 1956

classification.title: Proclamation Convening General Assembly in Extraordinary Session
classification.summary: This document is a proclamation by the Governor of Kentucky to convene the General Assembly in an extraordinary session on April 6, 1956. The sole purpose of this session is to consider and enact legislation relating to revenue and taxation to meet the Commonwealth's needs for the biennium beginning July 1, 1956, and ending June 30, 1958. This is in response to expenditures required by House Bill No. 1 of the Third Extraordinary Session of the 1956 General Assembly and other identified needs.

OF THE

PASSED AT THE EXTRAORDINARY SESSION OF THE GENERAL

CHAPTER 1
(S. B. 2)
AN ACT relating to annexation of unincorporated territory by munici-
palities in Kentucky .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
( 1 ) The General Assembly , recognizing that the general
welfare and prosperity of the Commonwealth of Kentucky is very
greatly dependent upon continued industrial development and
expansion; and , further recognizing that reasonable assurances
of fair treatment will greatly increase industrial development and
expansion in Kentucky , it hereby is declared to be the public
policy of the Commonwealth of Kentucky to encourage the loca-
tion of new industries and the expansion of existing industries in
Kentucky by prohibiting unfair and unreasonable annexation by
municipal corporations of industries now or hereafter located
in unincorporated areas . However , it is not the intent of the Gen-
eral Assembly to prohibit , restrict or hamper normal expansion of
municipal boundaries if such normal development and expansion
extends to and embraces such industrial properties .
(2 ) No unincorporated territory in which is located an
industrial plant or plants shall be annexed by any municipality un-
less such territory is embraced within a broad , comprehensive
plan of annexation . The territory to be annexed shall be contig-
uous to the boundary line or lines of such municipality , and the
territory or area to be annexed shall be both compact and con-
tiguous . The number of registered voters duly qualified to vote
in the territory proposed to be annexed shall equal or exceed
fifty ( 50 ) percent of the average number of persons employed
by industrial plants within such territory during the next pre-
ceding calendar year , the number of such registered voters

classification.title: AN ACT relating to annexation of unincorporated territory by municipalities in Kentucky.
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for the annexation of unincorporated industrial territories by municipalities in Kentucky. It aims to encourage industrial development by prohibiting unfair annexation practices, while allowing for normal municipal expansion. Specific criteria are set for annexation, including the territory being contiguous, compact, and having a sufficient number of registered voters relative to the number of people employed in industrial plants within the territory.

CHAPTER 3
(S. B. 7)
AN ACT relating to unemployment insurance .
Approved May 2 , 1956
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 341.350 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
Conditions of qualification for benefits . An unemployed
worker shall , except as provided in KRS 341.360 and 341.370 ,
be eligible for benefits with respect to any week of unemploy-
ment only if:
(1) He has made a claim for benefits ;
(2) He has registered for work with respect to such week
in accordance with regulations prescribed by the commission ;
(3) He is physically and mentally able to work ;
(4) He is available for suitable work , and is actively seek-
ing such work ;
(5) His base -period wages are not less than four hundred
and fifty dollars ; and
(6) He has served a waiting period of one week of un-
employment within his benefit year . No week of unemployment
shall be counted as a waiting period unless the worker has satis-
fied the conditions of subsections (1), (2), (3), (4) and (5) of this
section .
(7) The foregoing eligibility requirements and the con-
ditions of benefit disqualifications imposed by KRS 341.370 shall
be strictly construed .

These adjustments include exclusions for income exempt from state taxation, dividend income from in-state banks, federal income taxes, net operating losses, and personal exemptions. It also includes provisions for interest income from sister states and refunds of federal income tax. For individuals, it specifies how the standard deduction affects the definition of net income.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the definition of 'net income' for tax purposes in Kentucky. It outlines various adjustments and deductions related to federal and state income taxes, dividends, and other financial considerations for both individuals and corporations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

(20) "Number of withholding exemptions claimed " means

(21) "Wages " means "wages” as defined in Section 3401

(22) "Payroll period " means a period for which a payment
of wages is ordinarily made to the employe by his employer , and

(23) For the purpose of determining the maximum limita-
tions of medical and dental expense deduction :
(a) "Head of household " means head of household as de-

(b) " Surviving spouse " means "surviving spouse ” as de-
fined in Section 2 (b) of the Internal Revenue Code.
(24) "Corporations " includes associations and joint stock
companies .
(25) "Gross income" means "gross income ” as defined in
Section 61 of the Internal Revenue Code adjusted as follows :
(a) Exclude income that is exempt from state taxation by
the Constitution and statutory laws of the United States .
(b) Exclude dividend income received during the taxable
year upon stock of banks and trust companies organized under
the laws of this state .
(c) Include interest income derived from obligations of
sister states and political subdivisions thereof .
(d) Include refunds of federal income tax .
Partial exclusion of dividends received by individuals which
is authorized by Section 116 of the Internal Revenue Code shall
not be allowed .
Section 2. Section 141.020 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(1) With respect to taxable years beginning after December
31 , 1955 , an annual tax, consisting of a normal tax and a surtax ,
shall be paid for each taxable year by every resident individual
of this state , upon his entire net income as defined in this chapter .

classification.title: Kentucky Revised Statutes, Chapter 141 - Income Taxes
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, specifically KRS 141.020 as amended in 1956, outlines the annual income tax structure for residents. It defines terms related to withholding exemptions, wages, payroll periods, and deductions for medical and dental expenses, referencing the Internal Revenue Code for definitions. It establishes an annual tax consisting of a normal tax and a surtax on the entire net income of every resident individual for taxable years beginning after December 31, 1955. It also details adjustments to gross income, including exclusions for federally exempt income and dividends from state banks, and inclusions for interest from other states' obligations and federal income tax refunds.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to income tax regulations in Kentucky, specifically defining terms and establishing tax rates for individuals. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The definitions and tax structure are based on income and residency, not racial classification. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

except that if any individual who has attained the age of sixty-
five before the close of his taxable year shall be required to
make a return only if he has for the taxable year a gross income
of twelve hundred dollars or more . If the individual is unable
to make his own return , the return shall be made by a duly
authorized agent .
(2) An optional return properly filed under the provisions
of KRS 141.025 shall be deemed sufficient to satisfy the pro-
visions of this section .
(3) Each individual return shall be verified by a written
declaration that it is made under the penalties of perjury .
Section 10. Section 141.210 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
(1) As soon as practicable after each return is received , the
department shall examine and audit it. If the amount of tax
computed by the department is greater than the amount re-
turned by the taxpayer, the excess shall be assessed by the de-
partment within four years from the date the return was filed,
except as provided in subsection (2) of this section , and except
that in the case of a failure to file a return or of a fraudulent

at which food or drink is served and professional entertainers

classification.title: ACTS, FOURTH EXTRAORDINARY SESSION, 1956, PAGE 235
classification.summary: This section of the Acts of the Fourth Extraordinary Session of 1956 outlines tax exemptions for admissions to certain events. It specifies that the total charge for admission includes charges for seating or special facilities. Exemptions are provided for dramatic or musical productions by civic organizations in municipal parks if no pecuniary profit results to members and profits are used for civic, municipal, educational, charitable, or religious purposes. Further exemptions are granted for athletic contests, plays, concerts, or other amusements presented by nonprofit civic, fraternal, charitable, musical, social, or educational clubs or associations, provided no professional performers are employed and all profits are used for charitable, religious, or educational purposes within the state. However, no officer, employee, entertainer, member, or shareholder can receive pecuniary profit except for reasonable compensation for necessary services. If professional entertainers are employed, all admission amounts are taxed unless at least seventy-five percent of gross proceeds are used for charitable, religious, or educational purposes within the state. The intent is to impose the tax only once on sales of the same services.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses tax exemptions for admissions to various events, focusing on the conditions under which these exemptions apply, particularly for nonprofit and civic organizations. CHAPTER 11
(H. R. 4)
A JOINT RESOLUTION making an appropriation to the Legislative
Session Fund and declaring an emergency .
WHEREAS , the funds appropriated to the Legislative Session Fund for
the present biennium are almost exhausted, and
WHEREAS , present funds may not be adequate to pay members and
employees of the General Assembly for the duration of the extra-
ordinary sessions of the General Assembly ,
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That there is appropriated to the Legislative Ses-
sion Fund out of the General Expenditure Fund in the State
Treasury the sum of one hundred thousand dollars for the 1955-56
fiscal year .
Section 2. Whereas , funds previously appropriated to the
General Assembly are insufficient to meet the expenses of the
extraordinary sessions , an emergency is declared to exist , and this
Resolution shall become effective upon
itspassage and approval
by the Governor
.
Approved May
2, 1956

CHAPTER II
EXTRAORDINARY SESSION

CHAPTER II
EXTRAORDINARY SESSION

(a) A charge for seating or for special
admission to the legislative
chambers shall be deemed to include both the charge
for the present biennium and the charge
for the extra-ordinary session of the General Assembly.

WHEREAS, the present funds are not adequate to pay members
and employees of the General Assembly for the duration of the extra-
ordinary session of the General Assembly;

THEREFORE, NOW
BE IT RESOLVED by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

Section 1. That there is appropriated to the Legislative Ses-
sion Fund out of the General Expenditure Fund in the State
Treasury the sum of one hundred thousand dollars for the 1925-26
fiscal year.

Section 2. Whereas funds previously appropriated to the
General Assembly are insufficient to meet the expenses of the
extraordinary sessions an emergency is declared to exist and this
Resolution shall become effective upon its passage and approval
by the Governor.

Approved May 2, 1926

(b) If any professional entertainers or performers are
employed, all amounts paid for admission are subject to tax
unless at least seventy-five percent of the gross proceeds of
admissions are used for charitable, religious or educational pur-
poses within this state.

(c) It is the intent of this section to impose the tax once
only upon sales of the same services.

Approved April 27, 1926
